summary: MAT identifies a single large retained object graph: an ArrayList and its backing java.lang.Object[] together retain ~15.73 MB (≈94.84% of used heap). The retained graph is rooted in a local variable of the main thread and the allocation site is org.test.HeapDumper.main (HeapDumper.java:23). This indicates test/application code in org.test.HeapDumper.main is accumulating data in an in-memory collection and preventing it from being GC'd.

top_retained_objects.agent_note: Flagged by MAT as a leak suspect; retains the large backing Object[] referenced from HeapDumper.main.
Backing array for the ArrayList; holds the bulk of retained bytes and is referenced from the main thread.

dominant_allocator_stacks.leak_pattern: Large object graph accumulated in a local variable (ArrayList backing array) within main thread.

root_cause.description: A large java.lang.Object[] (backing an ArrayList) is retained by a local variable in org.test.HeapDumper.main, preventing GC.
root_cause.detailed_explanation: The main thread retains a reference to an ArrayList whose backing array holds ~15.73 MB of objects. Because the reference is kept in a local (or otherwise long-lived) variable in org.test.HeapDumper.main, the garbage collector cannot reclaim the large object graph. MAT shows the relevant stack frame at HeapDumper.java:23, indicating the data structure is populated or held in main and never released.

remediation: In org.test.HeapDumper.main - avoid accumulating the entire dataset in an in-memory ArrayList; process items streaming or write intermediate results to disk.
After processing, explicitly clear or null out the ArrayList (e.g., list.clear(); list = null;) so references are released and GC can reclaim the backing array.
If retention is intentional, consider using a bounded collection (e.g., EvictingQueue) or off-heap / disk-backed storage to limit heap usage; add tests to detect large-memory usage in CI.
